skill.name: team-collaboration-standup-notes
skill.description: Generate concise async standup notes from Git commits, Jira work, Obsidian notes, and calendar context.
skill.summary: Draft clear daily standup updates for remote and async teams.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command and blocker patterns, but most are documentation examples for standup generation workflows. The weak cryptography findings are false positives from ordinary text, while shell examples, temporary file usage, and optional Slack posting create moderate operational risk if copied into automation without review.

medium_findings.title: Executable Automation Examples Require Review
medium_findings.description: The playbook includes shell examples that call Git, Jira, Obsidian, calendar, Claude, and Slack tools. This is consistent with the skill purpose, but copied automation could read private work data and post it externally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically tied to legitimate standup generation. Risk depends on user execution and configured integrations, so this is not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Predictable Temporary File for Work Context
medium_findings.description: The sample script writes collected standup context to /tmp/standup-context.json. If used as written, this could expose Jira, calendar, commit, or note data through a predictable temporary path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary path is directly shown in the implementation script. The impact is moderate because it is example documentation, not installed executable code.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography matches occur in normal standup text such as description, design, descriptive, and priority wording. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage was found in those cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows ordinary prose and markdown examples, not cryptographic algorithms. The pattern likely matched substrings such as des in design or description.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Malicious Override
low_findings.description: The files contain normal skill instructions and prompt templates for generating standup notes. No evidence found of override commands, pre-approval claims, or instructions to skip security review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed instruction text stays within the stated communication workflow. It does not attempt to alter auditor behavior or claim special authority.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes shell commands and command substitutions for Git, Jira, calendar, and AI tooling. Users should review commands before running them in a real workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in a bash code block and includes multiple external commands. The context is instructional, which lowers severity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Scheduled Automation Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The cron example schedules daily execution of the generated script. Scheduled posting workflows should require human review before sharing team data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The crontab line is explicit, but it is presented as optional setup documentation. Human confirmation is included elsewhere in the sample script.

content.value_statement: Remote teams lose visibility when updates are scattered across commits, tickets, notes, and calendars. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code turn that context into clear daily standup notes.
actual_capabilities: Drafts Yesterday, Today, and Blockers sections for daily standups.
Suggests ways to summarize recent Git commits into team-facing accomplishments.
Provides Jira query patterns for completed, active, and planned work.
Uses Obsidian daily notes as context for tasks and meeting outcomes.
Offers async standup formats for Slack, Discord, email, and notes.
Includes blocker escalation templates with impact, need, owner, and next step.
limitations: Requires access to relevant Git, Jira, Obsidian, or calendar data from the user environment.
Generated notes need human review before posting to a team channel.
Example automation depends on local CLI tools that may not be installed.
It does not verify ticket status or calendar details without connected tools.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Daily Async Update
use_cases.description: Turn recent commits, notes, and active tickets into a concise standup post before the workday starts.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Blockers Across Teams
use_cases.description: Standardize blocker descriptions so owners, impact, and next actions are clear to distributed teams.

use_cases.title: Review Weekly Standup Patterns
use_cases.description: Use accumulated standup notes to identify recurring blockers, planning misses, and team support needs.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Basic Standup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a standup note with Yesterday, Today, and Blockers sections. Use these notes: [paste notes]. Keep each bullet concise.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Commit Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these recent commit messages and convert them into business-value standup bullets. Group related commits together: [paste commits].

prompt_templates.title: Merge Tickets and Notes
prompt_templates.prompt: Combine these Jira tickets, Obsidian tasks, and meeting notes into one standup update. Highlight blockers and planned outcomes: [paste context].

prompt_templates.title: Create Escalation-Ready Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a standup update for a blocked project. Include impact, owner, actions tried, workaround, and escalation timing: [paste details].

output_examples.input: Recent commits mention API authentication, a payment retry fix, and three PR reviews. Jira shows one profile ticket in progress.
output_examples.output: Yesterday: Shipped authentication work, fixed payment retry handling, and reviewed three teammate PRs. Today: Continue the profile ticket and prepare review notes. Blockers: None reported.

output_examples.input: A migration task is blocked by missing database access. The user has asked the database team and needs manager escalation.
output_examples.output: Blocker: Migration validation is blocked by missing database access. Impact: Production cutover risk remains unresolved. Need: Read-only access or sanitized staging data. Next step: Escalate today if access is not granted.

output_examples.input: Daily note lists two completed tasks, a design meeting decision, and a planned deployment window.
output_examples.output: Yesterday: Completed the listed tasks and captured the design decision. Today: Prepare deployment during the planned window. Notes: Include the decision link for reviewers.

best_practices: Review generated notes before sharing them with a team or external channel.
Link to tickets, pull requests, and documents instead of copying sensitive details.
Write blockers with a clear owner, needed action, impact, and deadline.
anti_patterns: Posting raw commit logs or private notes without summarizing sensitive details.
Using vague status words such as soon, later, or almost done.
Running the automation examples without checking local tools, permissions, and destinations.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically post standup notes?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflows and examples. Any posting depends on user-approved local tools and should include human review.

faq.question: Can it use Jira tickets?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Jira query patterns for completed, active, and planned work when Jira access is available.

faq.question: Can it work without Obsidian?
faq.answer: Yes. Obsidian adds useful context, but users can provide notes, tickets, commits, or meeting summaries directly.

faq.question: Is this only for software teams?
faq.answer: No. It is strongest for engineering teams, but the standup structure also fits product, design, and operations teams.

faq.question: What should users review before sharing output?
faq.answer: Users should check accuracy, remove sensitive details, confirm blockers, and verify links or ticket references.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill contains shell automation examples that can read local work data and post to Slack if users run them.
